blurb: Mount Rainier (14,406 ft, Pierce County, Washington) is a glaciated stratovolcano in the Cascade Range and the most heavily glaciated peak in the contiguous United States, carrying 26 major glaciers across roughly 30+ square miles of permanent snow and ice. Built of andesite and capped by the broad summit Columbia Crest with the satellite high points Liberty Cap (14,112 ft) and Point Success, it is a serious full-value alpine objective whose standard routes still gain about 9,000 vertical feet from the trailheads. The first ascent was made in August 1870 by Hazard Stevens and Philemon Beecher Van Trump via the Gibraltar Ledges line.

routes.overview: The Disappointment Cleaver (DC) is Mount Rainier's most popular line and the standard guided summit route, climbing the southeast side from Camp Muir over the Cowlitz and Ingraham glaciers and up the rock-and-snow cleaver to the east crater rim.
routes.beta: From Paradise (5,420 ft) ascend the Skyline Trail past Panorama Point and the Muir Snowfield to Camp Muir (~10,080 ft). On summit day cross the Cowlitz Glacier, traverse Cathedral Gap to Ingraham Flats (~11,200 ft), then ascend the Disappointment Cleaver to its top (~12,300 ft) — the route's halfway point and common turnaround decision — before weaving through crevasses and seracs to the crater rim and Columbia Crest. NPS reports an average summit success rate near 50%.
routes.approach: Drive to Paradise; hike the Skyline/Pebble Creek trail and the Muir Snowfield to Camp Muir (~4.5 mi, ~4,600 ft gain).
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent route, downclimbing the Cleaver and glaciers back to Camp Muir, then descend the Muir Snowfield to Paradise.
routes.descentText: Descend the same line: cross the crater, downclimb the upper mountain and the Disappointment Cleaver carefully (soft afternoon snow and rockfall on the Cleaver), recross Ingraham Flats and Cathedral Gap to Camp Muir, then glissade/walk the Muir Snowfield to Paradise. Mark the route with wands in poor visibility.
routes.itinerary: Common 2-3 day plan: Day 1 Paradise to Camp Muir (6-8 hr); Day 2 alpine start, summit and return to Camp Muir (8-12 hr round trip), then descend to Paradise (or descend Day 3).
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward to Camp Muir; the top of the Cleaver is the standard go/no-go point. From Camp Muir descend the Muir Snowfield to Paradise.
routes.turnaround: Pick a hard turnaround before leaving high camp based on weather, pace and snow conditions; many parties turn back if not above the Cleaver well before mid-morning so they descend the Cleaver before afternoon softening and rockfall. The top of the Cleaver (~12,300 ft) is the recognized decision point.
routes.bestSeason: Late May through early July offers the most reliable snow bridges and lowest rockfall; as summer progresses guides shift onto the Ingraham Direct, and after the route melts out rockfall and crevasses make the Cleaver hazardous.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage on most of the route; some signal is possible at Paradise, Camp Muir and high on the mountain. Carry a satellite communicator or PLB.
knownHazards: Crevasse falls and collapsing snow bridges across the Ingraham and Cowlitz glaciers.
Rockfall on and around the Disappointment Cleaver, especially after June as snow melts out.
Serac and icefall hazard weaving through the upper Ingraham above the Cleaver.
Whiteout navigation on the Muir Snowfield and exposure to high-altitude weather.
watchOut: Afternoon rockfall on the Cleaver — be off it early.
Hidden crevasses on the Ingraham Flats traverse.
Rapid weather changes and whiteout on the Muir Snowfield.
proTips: Acclimatize and move efficiently; success often hinges on an early alpine start.
Use wands on the Muir Snowfield for the descent in case of whiteout.
Monitor the freezing level — firm overnight snow is key to safe Cleaver travel.
routes.detailedRack: Standard glacier kit: rope, harness, crampons, mountaineering axe, a few pickets per rope team for running belays on steeper snow, and a full crevasse-rescue kit; helmet for the Cleaver rockfall zone.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is snow anchors (pickets) and team arrest; no rock or ice gear normally required on the standard line.

pitchDetail.notes: Skyline Trail and Muir Snowfield, ~4.5 mi to ~10,080 ft.
Cross Cowlitz Glacier and Cathedral Gap to ~11,200 ft camp.
Climb the cleaver to ~12,300 ft; rockfall-prone, halfway point.
Weave through crevasses/seracs to the crater rim, cross to Columbia Crest (14,406 ft).

timing.recommendedStart: Midnight to 2 a.m. alpine start from high camp

road.name: Paradise Road (SR 706 / Longmire–Paradise)
road.status: Open year-round to Paradise (winter access gated/seasonal hours)
road.seasonalGate: Longmire-to-Paradise gate closes nightly in winter and may close in storms
road.driveNote: Paid park entrance; plowed to Paradise (5,420 ft) — the standard trailhead.

climate.forecastZone: NWS Mount Rainier / mountain-forecast.com; NWAC Mount Rainier avalanche zone
climate.typical: High-altitude alpine: cold, wind and rapid weather changes possible any month; best window late spring to mid-summer.

bySeason.spring: Good snow coverage, firmer bridges, colder temps and avalanche awareness needed.
bySeason.summer: Prime season; rockfall and crevasses increase as snow melts.
bySeason.fall: Route melted out and dangerous; few attempts.
bySeason.winter: Severe weather, short days, full winter mountaineering conditions.

access.fees: $82/person annual Climbing Cost Recovery Fee; wilderness permit $12/person/night; park entrance fee separate
access.permit: Climbing permit required to travel on glaciers or above ~10,000 ft; wilderness/camp reservation required for Camp Muir/Ingraham
access.passRequired: Park entrance pass (America the Beautiful accepted)
access.closures: Seasonal road and facility closures; check NPS for current status

emergency.rangerStation: Mount Rainier NPS climbing information 360-569-6650; main park line 360-569-2211
emergency.nearestHospital: Arbor Health Morton Hospital (Morton, WA) for nearby care; Harborview Medical Center (Seattle) for major trauma
emergency.notes: Park rangers coordinate SAR with regional mountain rescue units and National Guard aviation; carry a PLB/satellite communicator.

routes.fa: Standard guided route on a peak first climbed Aug 17, 1870 by Hazard Stevens and P.B. Van Trump (via Gibraltar Ledges, not the DC)
routes.corrections: Peak first-ascent year is 1870; that ascent followed the Gibraltar Ledges line, not the Disappointment Cleaver, so the DC has no single 'first ascent' party. Summit now officially 14,406 ft (NAVD88, 2025); older sources cite 14,410-14,411 ft.

routes.overview: The Emmons-Winthrop Glacier is the standard non-DC route, climbing the broad northeast flank from Camp Schurman over the largest glacier in the contiguous U.S. — a less-crowded but longer alternative to the Disappointment Cleaver.
routes.beta: From the Glacier Basin Trailhead near White River Campground (~4,320 ft), hike the Glacier Basin Trail and ascend the Inter Glacier to Steamboat Prow and Camp Schurman (~9,460 ft). On summit day follow 'The Corridor' up the Emmons toward ~12,000 ft where the glacier becomes heavily crevassed, then weave through crevasses to the crater rim and Columbia Crest. The route gains over 10,000 ft over roughly 8 miles one way.
routes.approach: Glacier Basin Trail from White River Campground, then the Inter Glacier to Camp Schurman at Steamboat Prow (~9,460 ft).
routes.descent: Reverse the route to Camp Schurman, then descend the Inter Glacier and Glacier Basin Trail to White River.
routes.descentText: Downclimb the Emmons through the crevasse fields back to Camp Schurman, then descend the Inter Glacier (watch for crevasses and a glissade-worthy slope in good conditions) and hike the Glacier Basin Trail back to White River Campground.
routes.itinerary: Commonly 3-4 days: Day 1 White River to Camp Schurman (6-8 hr); Day 2 acclimatize/rest; Day 3 summit and return to camp; Day 4 descend to trailhead.
routes.bail: Retreat down 'The Corridor' to Camp Schurman is straightforward in good visibility; from camp descend the Inter Glacier to White River.
routes.turnaround: Set a turnaround before leaving Camp Schurman; firm overnight snow on the Inter and Emmons is essential, so descend the upper crevassed sections before strong afternoon warming softens bridges.
routes.bestSeason: Late May through July, with the most reliable snow bridges and crevasse crossings early; by late summer the Emmons becomes badly broken up.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage; a ranger is often stationed at Camp Schurman in season. Carry a satellite communicator or PLB.
knownHazards: Extensive crevasse fields on the upper Emmons above ~12,000 ft.
Collapsing snow bridges as the season warms.
Whiteout navigation across the broad, featureless glacier.
Falls on the Inter Glacier during approach/descent.
watchOut: Heavily crevassed terrain above The Corridor.
Soft afternoon bridges on descent.
Disorientation in cloud on the wide glacier — wand the route.
proTips: Wand the upper glacier for the descent.
Aim for firm pre-dawn snow on the Inter and Emmons.
Camp Schurman often has fewer crowds than Camp Muir.
routes.detailedRack: Glacier travel kit: rope, harness, crampons, axe, pickets for running belays, full crevasse-rescue system; helmet for the Inter Glacier and upper seracs.
routes.proNeeds: Snow protection (pickets) and team arrest; no technical rock/ice gear normally needed.

pitchDetail.notes: Glacier Basin Trail then Inter Glacier to ~9,460 ft.
Smooth snow slope up the Emmons to ~12,000 ft.
Weave crevasses/seracs to the crater rim and Columbia Crest.

timing.recommendedStart: Midnight to 2 a.m. from Camp Schurman

road.name: White River Road / SR 410 (Sunrise corridor)
road.status: Seasonal — typically open late spring to fall
road.seasonalGate: White River/Sunrise area roads close in winter and open early summer
road.driveNote: Drive to White River Campground / Glacier Basin Trailhead (~4,320 ft).

climate.forecastZone: NWS Mount Rainier / mountain-forecast.com; NWAC Mount Rainier avalanche zone
climate.typical: Alpine glacier climate; best stable window late spring to mid-summer.

bySeason.spring: Good coverage and firm bridges; cold.
bySeason.summer: Prime early summer; glacier breaks up by August.
bySeason.winter: Full winter conditions and closed approach roads.

access.fees: $82/person annual Climbing Cost Recovery Fee; wilderness permit $12/person/night; park entrance fee separate
access.permit: Climbing permit required; wilderness/camp reservation required for Camp Schurman/Emmons Flats
access.passRequired: Park entrance pass (America the Beautiful accepted)
access.closures: White River/Sunrise roads seasonal; check NPS

emergency.rangerStation: Camp Schurman ranger (seasonal); NPS climbing info 360-569-6650; main 360-569-2211
emergency.nearestHospital: Arbor Health Morton Hospital or Enumclaw-area care; Harborview Medical Center (Seattle) for major trauma
emergency.notes: Seasonal ranger presence at Camp Schurman; carry PLB/satellite communicator.

routes.corrections: High camp Camp Schurman is variously cited at 9,400-9,460 ft; sits at Steamboat Prow where the Emmons and Winthrop glaciers split.

routes.overview: The Kautz Glacier is a moderate technical alpine route on the south side, with its signature being a steep ice chute/headwall above Camp Hazard — a step up from the standard glacier routes.
routes.beta: From Paradise traverse west across the Nisqually Glacier and the Wilson/Turtle snowfields to Camp Hazard (~11,300 ft). The crux is the Kautz ice chute: descend a short gully past the ice cliff, then climb the ~300 ft headwall (two pitches with a 60 m rope) of 40-60° snow and ice, before continuing up the Kautz Glacier and upper mountain to the summit. Best done early season before the ice steepens and the route melts out.
routes.approach: From Paradise, traverse the Nisqually Glacier and Wilson/Turtle snowfields to high camp at Camp Hazard (~11,300 ft).
routes.descent: Descend the standard Disappointment Cleaver/Ingraham route to Camp Muir, or reverse the Kautz to Camp Hazard if conditions allow.
routes.descentText: Most parties descend the easier Disappointment Cleaver/Ingraham glacier route to Camp Muir and out to Paradise; reversing the Kautz ice chute is possible but committing. Coordinate a car/route plan for the different exit if descending the DC.
routes.itinerary: Typically 3 days: Day 1 Paradise to Camp Hazard; Day 2 climb the ice chute and summit, descend to camp or to Camp Muir; Day 3 hike out.
routes.bail: From below the ice chute, retreat back across the Nisqually/Wilson to Paradise; the chute itself is the committing section.
routes.turnaround: Time the ice chute for firm, cold conditions and set a turnaround so you are off the steep ice and across the Nisqually before afternoon warming raises rock/icefall risk.
routes.bestSeason: Late spring to early summer (roughly May-June); early season gives firmer, more continuous snow/ice in the chute and safer Nisqually crossing.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage; carry a satellite communicator or PLB.
knownHazards: Serac/icefall hazard from the Kautz ice cliff above the chute.
Steep 40-60° ice on the headwall requiring two-tool/ice-screw competence.
Crevasses on the Nisqually and Kautz glaciers.
Rockfall in the gully when melted out.
watchOut: Exposure to the overhanging ice cliff while accessing the chute — move quickly.
Hard ice in the chute late season.
Soft afternoon snow on the Nisqually crossing.
proTips: Climb the chute early and cold to minimize icefall exposure.
Bring a few ice screws and pickets for the headwall.
Plan the descent (often the DC) in advance.
routes.detailedRack: Glacier kit plus technical ice: a second tool, 3-4 ice screws and a couple of pickets for the ~300 ft ice headwall, plus full crevasse-rescue gear.
routes.proNeeds: Ice screws and pickets protect the chute/headwall; otherwise snow anchors and team arrest.

pitchDetail.notes: Nisqually + Wilson/Turtle snowfields to ~11,300 ft.
~300 ft headwall, two pitches with a 60 m rope; ice-cliff exposure.
Continue up the Kautz Glacier to the summit area.

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn alpine start from Camp Hazard

climate.forecastZone: NWS Mount Rainier / mountain-forecast.com; NWAC Mount Rainier
climate.typical: South-side alpine; best in firm early-season conditions.

access.fees: $82/person annual Climbing Cost Recovery Fee; wilderness permit $12/person/night; park entrance fee separate
access.permit: Climbing permit required; wilderness/camp permit for Camp Hazard
access.passRequired: Park entrance pass (America the Beautiful accepted)

emergency.nearestHospital: Arbor Health Morton Hospital; Harborview Medical Center (Seattle) for major trauma
emergency.notes: Carry PLB/satellite communicator; rangers coordinate SAR.

routes.corrections: Named for Lt. A.V. Kautz, whose 1857 attempt reached high on this side but did not summit; the glacier was established as a climbing route later. Grades cited as Grade II-III ice ~WI2 with sections of 40-60°.

routes.overview: Liberty Ridge splits Rainier's huge north face between the Willis Wall and Liberty Wall — a committing, serious snow-and-ice ridge regarded as one of the Fifty Classic Climbs of North America and one of the most dangerous routes on the mountain.
routes.beta: Approach via the Carbon Glacier to Thumb Rock camp (~10,760 ft). Above Thumb Rock, climb an ice gully or short steep ice band, then follow 45-60° snow/ice up the ridge past the Black Pyramid (~12,000 ft), traverse exposed faces, and climb several pitches of 40-60° ice and a bergschrund onto the Liberty Cap Glacier (~13,500 ft), topping out at Liberty Cap (14,112 ft). The route accounts for only ~2% of climbers but roughly 25% of fatalities on Rainier.
routes.approach: Long approach over the Carbon Glacier (from White River/Glacier Basin or Mowich) to the base of the ridge and Thumb Rock high camp (~10,760 ft).
routes.descent: Do not reverse the ridge; descend a standard route — typically the Emmons-Winthrop Glacier to Camp Schurman and White River.
routes.descentText: From Liberty Cap, traverse to the summit area and descend the Emmons-Winthrop Glacier to Camp Schurman, then the Inter Glacier and Glacier Basin Trail to White River. Reversing Liberty Ridge is not recommended; plan a through-trip with appropriate car/exit logistics.
routes.itinerary: Commonly 3-4 days: Day 1 trailhead to a Carbon Glacier/lower-ridge camp; Day 2 to Thumb Rock (~10,760 ft); Day 3 summit push over Liberty Cap and descend the Emmons; Day 4 out.
routes.bail: Bailing is hard and dangerous once on the ridge; the practical retreat is to keep climbing to Liberty Cap and descend the Emmons. Lower down, retreat across the Carbon Glacier.
routes.turnaround: This route demands cold, firm conditions and a fast pace; set an early turnaround and avoid being on the upper face during afternoon warming when rock and ice release — most fatalities involve falls and falling debris.
routes.bestSeason: Mid-May through June, when the ridge is well-covered in firm snow/ice and the Carbon Glacier crossing and Willis Wall icefall exposure are at their lowest; later season the route becomes icy, broken and more dangerous.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage on the remote north side; carry a satellite communicator or PLB.
knownHazards: Severe rockfall and icefall exposure from the Willis and Liberty walls flanking the ridge.
Steep 45-60° snow/ice with short steeper steps (70-80°) and a crux bergschrund near the top.
Crevasse and serac hazard on the Carbon and Liberty Cap glaciers.
Objective danger that gives this route a very high fatality rate.
watchOut: Be off exposed faces before afternoon warming releases debris.
The bergschrund/ice band onto the Liberty Cap Glacier can require short vertical ice.
Storms and whiteout on the committing upper face with no easy escape.
proTips: Go light and fast with a strong, experienced partner.
Aim for a hard freeze and stable high pressure.
Pre-arrange the Emmons descent and exit logistics.
routes.detailedRack: Light alpine technical rack: two ice tools, several ice screws, 2-3 pickets, a single rope (often 30-60 m for running belays and the bergschrund), full crevasse-rescue kit; some parties carry a couple of small rock pieces.
routes.proNeeds: Ice screws and pickets for running belays and the crux ice/bergschrund; mostly simul-climbing on steep snow/ice.

pitchDetail.notes: Approach to high camp at ~10,760 ft.
Ice gully or short 70-80° step, then ridge crest to ~12,000 ft.

pitchDetail.pitch: Black Pyramid to Liberty Cap Glacier
pitchDetail.notes: Traverse exposed east-face ice; cross bergschrund onto glacier ~13,500 ft.

pitchDetail.pitch: Liberty Cap Glacier to Liberty Cap
pitchDetail.notes: Top out at Liberty Cap (14,112 ft); continue to summit or descend Emmons.

timing.recommendedStart: Very early alpine start from Thumb Rock

road.name: White River Road / SR 410 (north-side access)
road.status: Seasonal — open late spring to fall
road.seasonalGate: White River/Sunrise roads gated in winter
road.driveNote: Access via White River/Glacier Basin (or Mowich) for the Carbon Glacier approach.

climate.forecastZone: NWS Mount Rainier / mountain-forecast.com; NWAC Mount Rainier
climate.typical: Cold, shaded north-face climate; needs a firm freeze for safety.

bySeason.spring: Best — firm snow/ice, lower debris risk.

access.fees: $82/person annual Climbing Cost Recovery Fee; wilderness permit $12/person/night; park entrance fee separate
access.permit: Climbing permit required; wilderness/camp permit for north-side camps
access.passRequired: Park entrance pass (America the Beautiful accepted)

emergency.nearestHospital: Enumclaw/Auburn-area hospitals; Harborview Medical Center (Seattle) for major trauma
emergency.notes: Remote north-side terrain — self-rescue capability essential; carry PLB/satellite communicator.

routes.fa: Ome Daiber, Arnold Campbell, Jim Borrow (Burrow), Sept 28 - Oct 1, 1935
routes.corrections: Route tops out at Liberty Cap (14,112 ft), not the true Columbia Crest summit; reaching the true summit requires additional glacier travel. Grade cited as IV (some sources V), ~AI3 with 45-60°+ ice.

routes.overview: The Fuhrer Finger is a classic south-side couloir line — a snow finger above the Wilson Glacier between rock walls — popular as a moderate alpine climb and one of Rainier's most-skied descents.
routes.beta: From Paradise traverse onto the Nisqually and Wilson glaciers and climb the namesake 'Finger', a narrow couloir of roughly 40-45° snow for about 2,200 ft, joining the upper Kautz Glacier route near ~13,000 ft and continuing to the summit. Its south aspect makes it a favored spring corn-snow ski line.
routes.approach: From Paradise, cross the Nisqually Glacier and ascend toward the base of the Finger above the Wilson Glacier.
routes.descent: Reverse the route (often skied) or descend the standard DC/Ingraham route to Camp Muir.
routes.descentText: In good spring conditions parties descend (ski) the Finger itself; otherwise descend the Kautz/upper mountain and the Disappointment Cleaver route to Camp Muir and Paradise.
routes.itinerary: Often done in 1-2 days: high camp near the base (~9,000-9,500 ft), then an alpine start up the Finger and summit, descending the same day.
routes.bail: Retreat down the couloir to the Wilson/Nisqually and back to Paradise; avoid the Finger during warm, active rockfall periods.
routes.turnaround: Climb the couloir early and cold; turn around and descend before the south-facing Finger warms and sheds rock and wet snow in late morning.
routes.bestSeason: April through June; the south aspect comes into spring corn and is most stable in the cool of early morning, with rockfall increasing as the couloir melts out.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage; carry a satellite communicator or PLB.
knownHazards: Significant rockfall funneling down the couloir, especially when warm.
Wet-snow/avalanche hazard on the south-facing Finger.
Crevasses on the Nisqually and upper glaciers.
Exposure to fast-changing weather high on the mountain.
watchOut: Be out of the Finger before it heats up and sheds rock.
Avalanche conditions after warming or new snow.
Hidden crevasses crossing the Nisqually.
proTips: Time it for firm overnight snow and an early start.
Treat the south aspect's warming as the main hazard clock.
Strong skiers often descend the Finger for a fast exit.
routes.detailedRack: Standard glacier kit with a couple of pickets for the steeper couloir; helmet essential for rockfall. Ski crampons useful for ski parties.
routes.proNeeds: Pickets and team arrest; no technical rock/ice gear normally required.

pitchDetail.notes: Across the Nisqually onto the Wilson Glacier.
~2,200 ft narrow couloir between rock walls to ~11,500 ft.
Join the upper Kautz route near ~13,000 ft to the summit.

climate.forecastZone: NWS Mount Rainier / mountain-forecast.com; NWAC Mount Rainier
climate.typical: South-side spring couloir conditions; warming-driven hazard.

bySeason.spring: Prime corn/firm snow early in the day.

access.fees: $82/person annual Climbing Cost Recovery Fee; wilderness permit $12/person/night; park entrance fee separate
access.permit: Climbing permit required; wilderness/camp permit if camping
access.passRequired: Park entrance pass (America the Beautiful accepted)

emergency.nearestHospital: Arbor Health Morton Hospital; Harborview Medical Center (Seattle) for major trauma

routes.fa: Hans Fuhrer, Heinie Fuhrer, Joseph Hazard, Peyton Farrer, July 2, 1920 (first ski descent May 3, 1980, Davis/Janisch/Haley)

routes.overview: The Fuhrer Thumb is the couloir just west of (and a steeper variation to) the Fuhrer Finger on Rainier's south side, climbing above the Wilson Glacier to join the upper mountain.
routes.beta: Like the Finger, approach from Paradise across the Nisqually onto the Wilson Glacier, then climb the 'Thumb' couloir — a slightly steeper, narrower snow line — to merge with the upper Kautz/Fuhrer terrain and continue to the summit. A south-facing spring snow climb and ski line.
routes.approach: From Paradise, cross the Nisqually Glacier to the Wilson Glacier headwall and the base of the Thumb couloir.
routes.descent: Descend the Fuhrer Finger or the standard DC/Ingraham route to Camp Muir.
routes.descentText: Most parties descend the adjacent Fuhrer Finger or the upper Kautz and standard Disappointment Cleaver route back to Camp Muir and Paradise.
routes.itinerary: Done in 1-2 days similar to the Finger, with an early start to climb the couloir cold.
routes.bail: Retreat down the couloir to the Wilson/Nisqually glaciers and back to Paradise.
routes.turnaround: Climb cold and early; descend before south-aspect warming triggers rockfall and wet-snow release.
routes.bestSeason: Spring (roughly April-June) for firm south-facing snow before the couloir melts out.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage; carry a satellite communicator or PLB.
knownHazards: Rockfall funneling through the steep, narrow couloir.
Wet-snow avalanche on the south aspect when warm.
Crevasses on the Nisqually and Wilson glaciers.
watchOut: Steeper, narrower than the Finger — more committing.
Be out of the couloir before warming.
Hidden crevasses on the approach.
proTips: Treat it as a steeper Finger variation and time it for a hard freeze.
Carry a picket or two for the steeper couloir.
routes.detailedRack: Glacier kit with pickets for the couloir; helmet essential for rockfall.
routes.proNeeds: Pickets and team arrest; no technical rock gear normally required.

climate.forecastZone: NWS Mount Rainier / mountain-forecast.com; NWAC Mount Rainier

access.fees: $82/person annual Climbing Cost Recovery Fee; wilderness permit $12/person/night; park entrance fee separate
access.permit: Climbing permit required; wilderness/camp permit if camping
access.passRequired: Park entrance pass (America the Beautiful accepted)

emergency.nearestHospital: Arbor Health Morton Hospital; Harborview Medical Center (Seattle) for major trauma

routes.overview: Gibraltar Ledges follows a ledge system around Gibraltar Rock on the south side — the line of Rainier's 1870 first ascent and now primarily a winter/early-season route because of severe rockfall once it melts out.
routes.beta: From Camp Muir (~10,080 ft) ascend the western edge of the Cowlitz Glacier below the Cowlitz Cleaver, pass beneath the Beehive, and follow the obvious ledge system across the face of Gibraltar Rock. The crux is an ice chute between Gibraltar Rock and the upper Nisqually ice cliff leading to the saddle, then ~2,000 ft of summit slopes to the east crater rim. Best when the ledges are firmly snowed/iced over in winter.
routes.approach: Standard Muir Snowfield approach from Paradise to Camp Muir (~10,080 ft).
routes.descent: Descend the Ingraham Direct/Disappointment Cleaver back to Camp Muir, or reverse the ledges if rockfall is low.
routes.descentText: Commonly descend the Ingraham Direct (through Cadaver Gap if needed) back to Camp Muir, avoiding re-exposure to the Gibraltar rockfall; then descend the Muir Snowfield to Paradise.
routes.itinerary: Often a 2-day winter/early-season climb: Paradise to Camp Muir, then a summit push and descent via the Ingraham Direct.
routes.bail: Retreat to Camp Muir along the ledges or via the Ingraham; the ice chute is the committing section.
routes.turnaround: Because rockfall is the dominant hazard, climb when the route is frozen and turn back before daytime warming loosens rock on the ledges and in the chute.
routes.bestSeason: Winter through early spring (roughly December-April), when snow/ice cover binds the ledges and rockfall is lowest; the route is not recommended in summer.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage; some signal possible at Camp Muir. Carry a satellite communicator or PLB.
knownHazards: Severe rockfall and icefall along the Gibraltar ledges and chute once melted out.
Steep ice in the Gibraltar Chute between the rock and the Nisqually ice cliff.
Loose 4th-class rock where snow cover is thin.
Exposure on the traverse ledges.
watchOut: Only climb when the ledges are well frozen/snow-covered.
Icefall from the Nisqually ice cliff near the chute.
Whiteout on the Cowlitz/upper slopes.
proTips: Treat it as a winter route — rockfall makes summer ascents inadvisable.
Carry pickets and a screw or two for the chute.
Plan an Ingraham descent to avoid re-crossing the ledges.
routes.detailedRack: Glacier kit plus a couple of pickets and an ice screw or two for the Gibraltar Chute; helmet mandatory for the rockfall-prone ledges.
routes.proNeeds: Pickets along the ledges and a screw/two in the ice chute; team arrest elsewhere.

pitchDetail.notes: Cowlitz Glacier edge below the Cowlitz Cleaver, past the Beehive.
Traverse the ledge system across the face of Gibraltar Rock.
Ice chute between rock and the Nisqually ice cliff to the saddle.
~2,000 ft to the east crater rim and summit.

road.name: Paradise Road (SR 706)
road.seasonalGate: Longmire-Paradise gate closed nightly in winter
road.driveNote: Winter access to Paradise (5,420 ft) for this cold-season route.

climate.forecastZone: NWS Mount Rainier / mountain-forecast.com; NWAC Mount Rainier
climate.typical: Best as a cold-season route when the face is frozen.

bySeason.spring: Early spring still good while frozen.
bySeason.winter: Prime when snow/ice bind the ledges, but full winter conditions.

access.fees: $82/person annual Climbing Cost Recovery Fee; wilderness permit $12/person/night; park entrance fee separate
access.permit: Climbing permit required; wilderness/camp permit for Camp Muir
access.passRequired: Park entrance pass (America the Beautiful accepted)

emergency.nearestHospital: Arbor Health Morton Hospital; Harborview Medical Center (Seattle) for major trauma

routes.fa: Line of the first ascent of Mount Rainier, Aug 17, 1870 (Hazard Stevens and P.B. Van Trump)

routes.overview: The Tahoma Glacier is a long west-side route, an early-season moderate glacier climb reached from the Westside Road that ascends Rainier's western flank past the Tahoma icefall toward the summit.
routes.beta: From the Westside Road gate (~2,900 ft), hike the road to the Tahoma Creek/South Puyallup trails and ascend onto the upper Tahoma Glacier, which cascades as an icefall from ~13,200 ft to ~11,000 ft. The route weaves 30-45° glacier terrain to the summit and is best done early season before the icefall and crevasses open up.
routes.approach: Westside Road gate (~2,900 ft) to Tahoma Creek/Wonderland trails, ascending to ~5,600 ft and onto the glacier.
routes.descent: Reverse the route, or descend an adjacent west-side line; navigate the icefall carefully.
routes.descentText: Descend the Tahoma Glacier, threading the icefall/crevasses on the same line; in poor visibility the broad west-side glaciers demand careful navigation back to the trail and Westside Road.
routes.itinerary: Multi-day: hike the road and approach to a high glacier camp, then summit and descend, with the long road walk on the exit.
routes.bail: Retreat down the glacier to the approach trails and the Westside Road.
routes.turnaround: Cross the icefall and crevassed sections when firm; turn back before afternoon warming weakens bridges on the long west-side glacier.
routes.bestSeason: Late spring to early summer (roughly May-June) when snow bridges are intact and the icefall is more passable; the route deteriorates as summer progresses.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage on the remote west side; carry a satellite communicator or PLB.
knownHazards: Crevasses and seracs through the Tahoma icefall (~11,000-13,200 ft).
Navigation challenges on the broad, remote west-side glaciers.
Long approach with the gated Westside Road adding mileage.
Whiteout exposure high on the mountain.
watchOut: Icefall passage can be complex and changes year to year.
Long self-sufficient outing far from help.
Soft afternoon bridges on descent.
proTips: Go early season for the best icefall passage.
Budget for the long Westside Road walk.
Wand the upper glacier for the descent.
routes.detailedRack: Glacier kit with pickets and a couple of screws for steeper icefall sections; full crevasse-rescue gear for the complex glacier.
routes.proNeeds: Pickets and screws for the icefall; team arrest elsewhere.

road.name: Westside Road
road.status: Gated to vehicles at ~2,900 ft (closed beyond the gate)
road.seasonalGate: Vehicle gate near the start; the road beyond is hiked/biked
road.driveNote: From the Nisqually Entrance turn north onto Westside Road and park at the gate (~2,900 ft).

climate.forecastZone: NWS Mount Rainier / mountain-forecast.com; NWAC Mount Rainier
climate.typical: Remote west-side alpine; best early-season firm conditions.

access.fees: $82/person annual Climbing Cost Recovery Fee; wilderness permit $12/person/night; park entrance fee separate
access.permit: Climbing permit required; wilderness/camp permit for west-side camps
access.passRequired: Park entrance pass (America the Beautiful accepted)
access.closures: Westside Road gated to vehicles; check NPS

emergency.nearestHospital: Arbor Health Morton Hospital; Harborview Medical Center (Seattle) for major trauma
emergency.notes: Remote west side — self-sufficiency essential; carry PLB/satellite communicator.

routes.overview: Ptarmigan Ridge is a committing north-side alpine route between the Mowich Face and the North Face, climbing steep snow and ice past the Ptarmigan ice cliffs to Liberty Cap.
routes.beta: After a long north-side approach over St. Elmo Pass, the Winthrop and Carbon glaciers, follow the ridge to a high camp around ~10,200 ft below the Ptarmigan ice cliffs. The route then climbs 45-55° (often icy) snow/ice — by the rock buttress and around the icefall — with AI/WI2-3 and short rock/mixed steps, topping out near Liberty Cap (14,112 ft).
routes.approach: From the Mowich/north side via St. Elmo Pass (~7,600 ft), traverse the Winthrop Glacier to Curtis Ridge, cross the Carbon Glacier (~7,000 ft), and gain Ptarmigan Ridge to high camp (~10,200 ft).
routes.descent: Descend a standard route — typically the Emmons-Winthrop Glacier to Camp Schurman and White River.
routes.descentText: From Liberty Cap traverse toward the summit and descend the Emmons-Winthrop Glacier to Camp Schurman and the White River trailhead; reversing Ptarmigan Ridge is not practical.
routes.itinerary: 3-4 days: long approach to high camp, a summit day over the ice cliffs to Liberty Cap, then descent via the Emmons.
routes.bail: Bailing from the upper ridge is difficult; lower down retreat across the Carbon Glacier toward the approach.
routes.turnaround: Demands cold, firm conditions; set an early turnaround and avoid the ice cliffs and steep faces during afternoon warming.
routes.bestSeason: Roughly June-July when the north-side approach is passable and the ridge holds firm snow/ice; conditions vary year to year.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage on the remote north side; carry a satellite communicator or PLB.
knownHazards: Icefall from the Ptarmigan ice cliffs above high camp.
Sustained 45-55° icy snow/ice with short mixed steps.
Crevasse and serac hazard on the Carbon and Winthrop glaciers.
Remote, committing terrain with no easy escape.
watchOut: Time passage of the ice cliffs for cold conditions.
Long complex approach with multiple glacier crossings.
Storms on the exposed upper ridge.
proTips: Go light and fast with strong glacier and ice skills.
Pre-arrange the Emmons descent and exit.
Aim for a hard freeze and stable weather.
routes.detailedRack: Light alpine technical rack: two tools, several ice screws, pickets, a single rope for running belays, full crevasse-rescue kit.
routes.proNeeds: Ice screws and pickets for the steep ice/cliffs; simul-climbing on moderate snow/ice.

pitchDetail.notes: St. Elmo Pass, Winthrop/Carbon glaciers to ~10,200 ft below ice cliffs.
Climb past the rock buttress and around the icefall, often icy.
Top out near Liberty Cap (14,112 ft).

timing.recommendedStart: Very early alpine start from high camp

road.name: Mowich Lake Road / White River (north-side access)
road.status: Seasonal — typically open early summer to fall
road.seasonalGate: Mowich Lake Road and Sunrise/White River roads gated in winter
road.driveNote: Access the north side via Mowich Lake or White River; long approach to the ridge.

bySeason.spring: Approach often still snowbound; deep snow.

routes.fa: First ascent 1935 (Fritz Wiessner and William House)
routes.corrections: Route tops out at Liberty Cap (14,112 ft), not the true Columbia Crest summit. Commonly graded IV, AI2-3 with 45-55° snow/ice and short mixed steps.

routes.overview: Sunset Ridge climbs the ridge above the Sunset Amphitheater on Rainier's west/northwest side, separating the upper Mowich Face from the amphitheater — a long, remote, mostly non-technical-but-committing line to Liberty Cap.
routes.beta: From the Westside Road, climb past Tokaloo Rock and traverse the Puyallup and South Mowich glaciers to a bivouac around ~8,500 ft, then climb steep snow up the face of the ridge through a series of gullies and snowfields above the Sunset Amphitheater, topping out near Liberty Cap (14,112 ft).
routes.approach: Westside Road gate (~2,900 ft) to Round Pass and the St. Andrews/Tokaloo area, then traverse the Puyallup/South Mowich glaciers to ~8,500 ft.
routes.descent: Descend a standard route, typically traversing to the Emmons-Winthrop or reversing toward the west-side glaciers.
routes.descentText: From Liberty Cap descend via the summit area to the Emmons-Winthrop Glacier, or descend west-side glaciers back toward the Westside Road; plan exit logistics for the long approach.
routes.itinerary: Multi-day: long road/glacier approach to a high bivy, a summit day up the ridge to Liberty Cap, then descent and the long exit.
routes.bail: Retreat down the ridge and west-side glaciers toward the approach bivy and Westside Road.
routes.turnaround: On the long, committing upper ridge, set an early turnaround and avoid the steep gullies and amphitheater rim during afternoon warming and serac/rockfall.
routes.bestSeason: Late spring to early summer for the best snow on the long approach and ridge.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage on the remote west side; carry a satellite communicator or PLB.
knownHazards: Serac and icefall hazard from the Sunset Amphitheater walls.
Crevasses on the Puyallup/South Mowich glaciers.
Long, remote, navigation-intensive approach.
Storm exposure on the upper ridge.
watchOut: Avoid the amphitheater fall-line under seracs.
Complex route-finding through gullies and snowfields.
Self-sufficient outing far from help.
proTips: Budget for the long Westside Road and glacier approach.
Go early season for firm snow.
Wand key turns for the descent.
routes.detailedRack: Glacier kit with pickets for steep snow; a screw or two if icy; full crevasse-rescue gear for the long glaciated approach.
routes.proNeeds: Pickets and team arrest on steep snow; minimal technical pro otherwise.

road.name: Westside Road
road.seasonalGate: Vehicle gate near the start; road beyond is hiked
road.driveNote: From the Nisqually Entrance take Westside Road to the gate (~2,900 ft); ~3.5 mi road walk to Round Pass.

climate.typical: Remote west-side alpine; best early-season firm snow.

bySeason.spring: Good coverage on approach and ridge.

routes.corrections: Tops out near Liberty Cap (14,112 ft), not the true summit.

routes.overview: The Mowich Face is a steep, remote ice-and-mixed wall on Rainier's northwest side, offering several lines of sustained 40-55° ice with sections of rock to Liberty Cap — a serious alpine objective.
routes.beta: Approached from Mowich Lake over the northwest glaciers, the face rises roughly 4,000 ft of sustained 40-55° (Grade IV) ice with some 5th-class rock to the Liberty Cap area (~14,112 ft). Multiple lines (Central Mowich Face, Edmunds Headwall, etc.) share the wall and demand efficient ice/mixed climbing.
routes.approach: From Mowich Lake (northwest corner of the park), descend/traverse to the South Mowich/Edmunds glaciers and the base of the face.
routes.descent: Descend a standard route — commonly traverse to the Emmons-Winthrop Glacier after topping out near Liberty Cap.
routes.descentText: From the Liberty Cap area descend toward the summit and the Emmons-Winthrop Glacier to Camp Schurman and White River, or descend west-side glaciers back toward Mowich; plan exit logistics in advance.
routes.itinerary: 3-4 days: approach from Mowich to a base camp, climb the face in a long summit day to Liberty Cap, then descend a standard route.
routes.bail: Retreat off the steep face is difficult; lower down, descend the approach glaciers toward Mowich.
routes.turnaround: On a sustained 4,000 ft ice face, climb in cold firm conditions and set an early turnaround to avoid afternoon rock/icefall and softening.
routes.bestSeason: Roughly June-July when the face holds firm ice and the northwest approach is passable; conditions vary year to year.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage on the remote northwest side; carry a satellite communicator or PLB.
knownHazards: Sustained ~4,000 ft of 40-55° ice with 5th-class rock sections.
Rockfall and icefall down the face, especially when warm.
Crevasses on the approach glaciers.
Remote, committing terrain with hard retreat.
watchOut: Be efficient on the long ice face and off it before warming.
Mixed rock steps requiring careful protection.
Self-sufficient outing far from help.
proTips: Strong ice and mixed skills and a fast pace are essential.
Pre-arrange the Emmons descent and exit.
Aim for a hard freeze and stable weather.
routes.detailedRack: Technical alpine rack: two tools, a set of ice screws, pickets, a light rock rack for the mixed sections, a single rope for belays/simul, full crevasse-rescue kit.
routes.proNeeds: Ice screws on the face, light rock pro for mixed steps, pickets on moderate snow.

pitchDetail.notes: From Mowich Lake to the South Mowich/Edmunds glaciers.
~4,000 ft sustained ice/mixed wall.
Top out near Liberty Cap (14,112 ft).

timing.recommendedStart: Very early alpine start from base camp

emergency.notes: Remote northwest side; carry PLB/satellite communicator.

routes.corrections: Tops out near Liberty Cap (14,112 ft), not the true Columbia Crest summit. Mowich Face holds several distinct lines; graded ~III-IV/IV-V depending on line.

routes.overview: Curtis Ridge is one of the big north-side ridge routes, considered technically harder, more confusing and more committing than either Liberty Ridge or Ptarmigan Ridge — an excellent hard line for fast, competent parties in good cold conditions.
routes.beta: From the north side, approach over the Winthrop/Carbon glaciers to the base of Curtis Ridge, then climb the long, complex ridge of steep snow, ice and mixed terrain toward the upper mountain and Liberty Cap (14,112 ft). Route-finding is intricate and the line is sustained and committing.
routes.approach: North-side approach (Sunrise/White River via St. Elmo Pass and the Winthrop/Carbon glaciers) to the toe of Curtis Ridge.
routes.descent: Descend a standard route, typically the Emmons-Winthrop Glacier to Camp Schurman and White River.
routes.descentText: After topping out near Liberty Cap, descend toward the summit and the Emmons-Winthrop Glacier to Camp Schurman and White River; reversing the ridge is impractical.
routes.itinerary: 3-4 days: approach to a ridge/bivy camp, a long summit day up the ridge, then descent via the Emmons.
routes.bail: Retreat off the upper ridge is difficult; lower down descend the Carbon/Winthrop glaciers toward the approach.
routes.turnaround: On this committing, route-finding-heavy ridge, climb in cold firm conditions and set an early turnaround to avoid being benighted or caught by afternoon instability.
routes.bestSeason: Roughly June-July in good cold conditions; the route is best for fast competent parties when firm.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage on the remote north side; carry a satellite communicator or PLB.
knownHazards: Complex, sustained ridge with intricate route-finding.
Steep snow/ice and mixed terrain, more committing than neighboring ridges.
Crevasse, serac and rockfall hazard on the Carbon/Winthrop approach and ridge.
Remote north-side position with hard retreat.
watchOut: Easy to get cliffed-out or benighted in poor route-finding.
Afternoon instability on the steep ridge.
Storms with no easy escape.
proTips: Best for fast, experienced parties moving light.
Study the line carefully and aim for a hard freeze.
Pre-arrange the Emmons descent and exit.
routes.detailedRack: Technical alpine rack: two tools, ice screws, pickets, a light rock rack for mixed steps, a single rope, full crevasse-rescue kit.
routes.proNeeds: Mixed protection — screws, pickets and light rock gear — with simul-climbing on moderate terrain.

road.name: White River Road / Sunrise (SR 410)
road.status: Seasonal — open late spring to fall
road.seasonalGate: Sunrise/White River roads gated in winter
road.driveNote: Access the north side via White River/Sunrise for the Curtis Ridge approach.

emergency.notes: Remote north side; carry PLB/satellite communicator.

routes.corrections: Tops out near Liberty Cap (14,112 ft), not the true Columbia Crest summit.

routes.overview: Willis Wall is Rainier's notorious ~3,600 ft north-face wall beneath Liberty Cap, capped by a near-continuous summit ice cliff that releases huge avalanches — among the most dangerous and rarely climbed objectives on the mountain.
routes.beta: Reached via the Carbon Glacier, the wall rises in consistently 45-55° (locally ~60°) snow and ice beneath the Liberty Cap summit ice cliff, with multiple rib lines. The defining hazard is the overhanging summit ice cliff, which calved debris sweeps the face; climbers aim for ribs (e.g., the West Rib) and speed to limit exposure, topping out near Liberty Cap (14,112 ft).
routes.approach: From the north side, approach the Carbon Glacier to the base of Willis Wall below the Liberty Cap ice cliff.
routes.descent: Descend a standard route, typically the Emmons-Winthrop Glacier after topping out at Liberty Cap.
routes.descentText: From Liberty Cap descend toward the summit and the Emmons-Winthrop Glacier to Camp Schurman and White River. Reversing the wall is not an option.
routes.itinerary: Multi-day, weather-dependent: approach and bivy below the wall, then a fast, committing summit-day push up a rib to Liberty Cap and a standard-route descent.
routes.bail: There is essentially no safe retreat off the wall once committed; the practical 'escape' is to climb out to Liberty Cap.
routes.turnaround: Exposure to the summit ice cliff is constant; the route must be climbed fast in cold conditions, and parties commonly climb at night/early to minimize time under the cliff rather than relying on a clock turnaround.
routes.bestSeason: Cold/firm conditions (winter and early season) when the snow/ice is bonded and ice-cliff activity is lower; still extremely hazardous.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage on the remote north side; carry a satellite communicator or PLB.
knownHazards: Near-continuous summit ice cliff that releases large avalanches sweeping the wall.
Consistently 45-55° (to ~60°) snow and ice for ~3,600 ft.
Rockfall and icefall throughout the face.
Essentially no safe retreat once committed.
watchOut: The summit ice cliff is the dominant, objective danger — minimize time beneath it.
Loose rock and ice between the ribs.
Storms with no escape on a huge committing wall.
proTips: Climb fast, light and cold; pick a rib that limits ice-cliff exposure.
Many strong parties consider the objective hazard unacceptable in warm conditions.
Pre-arrange the Emmons descent and exit.
routes.detailedRack: Light, fast technical kit: two tools, ice screws, pickets, a single rope for simul/belays, full crevasse-rescue gear; speed is the main 'protection'.
routes.proNeeds: Ice screws and pickets for the steep ice; mostly simul-climbing to move fast under the ice cliff.

timing.recommendedStart: Night/very early to limit ice-cliff exposure

road.name: White River / Mowich (north-side access)
road.status: Seasonal — open late spring to fall (winter approaches are long)
road.driveNote: Access via White River or Mowich for the Carbon Glacier approach.

bySeason.spring: Early season firmer but still extreme.

routes.fa: Charles Bell, June 1961 (West Rib; solo, disputed at the time)
routes.corrections: Tops out near Liberty Cap (14,112 ft). The wall is ~3,600 ft; Bell's 1961 solo first ascent of the West Rib was initially doubted but is now generally credited.

routes.overview: The Edmunds Headwall is a steep northwest-side ice line dropping off Liberty Cap, part of the Mowich/Edmunds wall complex — plumb, sustained and steep from ~13,400 ft down to ~9,900 ft.
routes.beta: Approached from Mowich Lake over the Edmunds/South Mowich glaciers, the headwall climbs sustained steep snow/ice (commonly ~45-55°) beneath Liberty Cap. From roughly 9,900 ft to ~13,400 ft it is direct and sustained, topping out at Liberty Cap (14,112 ft); it is also a noted steep ski line (first skied 1997).
routes.approach: From Mowich Lake, traverse to the Edmunds/South Mowich glaciers and the base of the headwall.
routes.descent: Descend a standard route, typically traversing to the Emmons-Winthrop Glacier after topping out at Liberty Cap.
routes.descentText: From Liberty Cap descend toward the summit and the Emmons-Winthrop Glacier to Camp Schurman and White River, or descend west-side glaciers toward Mowich; plan exit logistics.
routes.itinerary: 3-4 days: approach from Mowich to a base camp, climb the headwall in a summit day to Liberty Cap, then descend a standard route.
routes.bail: Retreat off the steep headwall is difficult; lower down descend the approach glaciers toward Mowich.
routes.turnaround: On the sustained headwall, climb in cold firm conditions and set an early turnaround to avoid afternoon rock/icefall and softening.
routes.bestSeason: Roughly June-July when the face holds firm ice and the northwest approach is passable.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage on the remote northwest side; carry a satellite communicator or PLB.
knownHazards: Sustained steep snow/ice (~45-55°) from ~9,900 to ~13,400 ft.
Rockfall and icefall down the headwall when warm.
Crevasses on the Edmunds/South Mowich approach.
Remote, committing position.
watchOut: Be efficient and off the face before warming.
Icefall from above near Liberty Cap.
Self-sufficient outing far from help.
proTips: Strong ice skills and a fast pace; aim for a hard freeze.
Noted steep ski descent for expert ski-mountaineers.
Pre-arrange the Emmons descent and exit.
routes.detailedRack: Technical alpine kit: two tools, ice screws, pickets, a single rope for simul/belays, full crevasse-rescue gear.
routes.proNeeds: Ice screws and pickets on the steep face; simul-climbing on moderate terrain.

timing.recommendedStart: Very early alpine start from base camp

road.driveNote: Drive the gravel Mowich Lake Road to Mowich Lake (northwest corner).

routes.corrections: Tops out near Liberty Cap (14,112 ft), not the true Columbia Crest summit. First ski descent 1997 (DuBuque, Ingersoll, McLean, Skoog).

routes.overview: The Kautz Headwall (Wilson/Wapowety Cleaver headwall) is a steep south-side snow-and-ice face left of the Kautz Glacier, climbing moderately steep 30-50° snow/ice through couloirs to the upper mountain.
routes.beta: Accessed off the northwest head of the Wilson Glacier through narrow couloirs onto the headwall, the climb takes 30-50° snow and ice up the central/western side of Wapowety Cleaver, topping out around ~13,300 ft before the summit slopes. There is substantial exposure to icefall from the Kautz Ice Cliff while accessing the headwall.
routes.approach: From Paradise, traverse the Nisqually and Wilson glaciers to the base of the headwall/couloirs.
routes.descent: Descend the standard Disappointment Cleaver/Ingraham route to Camp Muir, or the Kautz Glacier.
routes.descentText: Most parties descend the standard DC/Ingraham route to Camp Muir and Paradise, avoiding re-exposure to the Kautz Ice Cliff; coordinate exit logistics.
routes.itinerary: Typically 2-3 days: high camp on the south side, an alpine start up the headwall, and a standard-route descent.
routes.bail: Retreat back across the Wilson/Nisqually to Paradise; the couloir access under the ice cliff is the committing part.
routes.turnaround: Time the ice-cliff exposure for cold conditions and set a turnaround so you are off the headwall and clear of the Kautz Ice Cliff before afternoon warming.
routes.bestSeason: Late spring to early summer for firm snow/ice on the headwall and a safer approach.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage; carry a satellite communicator or PLB.
knownHazards: Substantial icefall exposure from the Kautz Ice Cliff when accessing the headwall.
Moderately steep 30-50° snow and ice through narrow couloirs.
Crevasses on the Wilson/Nisqually glaciers.
Rockfall where the cleaver is melted out.
watchOut: Minimize time beneath the Kautz Ice Cliff.
Hard ice on the headwall late season.
Hidden crevasses on the approach.
proTips: Climb the access couloirs early and cold.
Carry screws and pickets for the headwall.
Plan a standard-route descent.
routes.detailedRack: Glacier kit plus technical ice: a second tool, ice screws and pickets for the headwall; full crevasse-rescue gear.
routes.proNeeds: Ice screws and pickets on the headwall; team arrest elsewhere.

climate.forecastZone: NWS Mount Rainier / mountain-forecast.com; NWAC Mount Rainier
climate.typical: South-side alpine; best early-season firm conditions.

access.fees: $82/person annual Climbing Cost Recovery Fee; wilderness permit $12/person/night; park entrance fee separate
access.permit: Climbing permit required; wilderness/camp permit if camping
access.passRequired: Park entrance pass (America the Beautiful accepted)

emergency.nearestHospital: Arbor Health Morton Hospital; Harborview Medical Center (Seattle) for major trauma

routes.corrections: Often described as the Wilson/Wapowety Cleaver headwall; 30-50° snow/ice topping out around ~13,300 ft with significant Kautz Ice Cliff exposure.

routes.overview: The Nisqually Icefall is a steep technical south-side ice line up the broken Nisqually Glacier above Paradise, featuring large icefalls and crevasses — an early-season ice objective rather than a casual glacier route.
routes.beta: From Paradise, climb onto the Nisqually Glacier and ascend through its icefall, which holds large seracs and crevasses, to gain the upper mountain. It is a committing ice route demanding screws and ice-climbing competence, climbed best early season when bridges are firm.
routes.approach: From Paradise, drop onto and ascend the Nisqually Glacier toward the icefall.
routes.descent: Descend the standard Disappointment Cleaver/Ingraham route to Camp Muir, or an adjacent south-side line.
routes.descentText: Most parties descend a standard route (DC/Ingraham to Camp Muir) rather than reversing the icefall; plan exit logistics back to Paradise.
routes.itinerary: 1-2 days with an early start to climb the icefall in firm, cold conditions.
routes.bail: Retreat down the glacier toward Paradise if the icefall is impassable or unstable.
routes.turnaround: Climb the icefall cold and early; turn back before warming destabilizes seracs and bridges.
routes.bestSeason: Early season (spring) when bridges and ice are firm and the icefall is more passable.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage; carry a satellite communicator or PLB.
knownHazards: Large seracs and icefall that can collapse without warning.
Extensive crevasses and complex, shifting route-finding.
Steep ice requiring screws and ice technique.
Exposure to south-side warming.
watchOut: Serac collapse is the dominant hazard — minimize exposure.
Route changes year to year as the icefall shifts.
Soft afternoon bridges.
proTips: Go early season and cold for the firmest, most passable ice.
Carry ample ice screws.
Move fast through the serac fall-lines.
routes.detailedRack: Technical ice/glacier kit: two tools, a rack of ice screws, pickets, full crevasse-rescue gear for the heavily broken glacier.
routes.proNeeds: Ice screws for the steep ice and pickets/team arrest on snow.

climate.forecastZone: NWS Mount Rainier / mountain-forecast.com; NWAC Mount Rainier
climate.typical: South-side icefall; best early season when cold and firm.

access.fees: $82/person annual Climbing Cost Recovery Fee; wilderness permit $12/person/night; park entrance fee separate
access.permit: Climbing permit required; wilderness/camp permit if camping
access.passRequired: Park entrance pass (America the Beautiful accepted)

emergency.nearestHospital: Arbor Health Morton Hospital; Harborview Medical Center (Seattle) for major trauma

routes.overview: The Ingraham Direct (ID) is the most direct standard line from Camp Muir up the Ingraham Glacier to the crater rim — the early-season standard and guided route before the Disappointment Cleaver comes into condition.
routes.beta: From Camp Muir (~10,080 ft), cross the Cowlitz Glacier and Cathedral Gap to Ingraham Flats (~11,200 ft), then climb the Ingraham Glacier directly through crevasse and serac terrain to the east crater rim and Columbia Crest. Guides favor the ID in early season when snow bridges are intact, switching to the Disappointment Cleaver as the ID melts out and seracs become hazardous.
routes.approach: Standard Muir Snowfield approach from Paradise to Camp Muir (~10,080 ft).
routes.descent: Reverse the route to Camp Muir, then descend the Muir Snowfield to Paradise.
routes.descentText: Downclimb the Ingraham Glacier back through the crevasse fields to Ingraham Flats and Camp Muir, then descend the Muir Snowfield to Paradise; wand the route in poor visibility.
routes.itinerary: Common 2-day plan: Day 1 Paradise to Camp Muir (6-8 hr); Day 2 alpine start, summit and return to Camp Muir, then descend to Paradise.
routes.bail: Retreat to Ingraham Flats and Camp Muir is straightforward in good visibility; from camp descend to Paradise.
routes.turnaround: Set a hard turnaround before leaving Camp Muir; climb the crevassed/serac sections when firm and descend before afternoon warming weakens bridges.
routes.bestSeason: Early season (roughly May-June) when the Ingraham's snow bridges are intact and seracs are stable; the route is abandoned for the DC once it melts out.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage on most of the route; some signal possible at Camp Muir and high on the mountain. Carry a satellite communicator or PLB.
knownHazards: Serac fall hazard on the direct Ingraham line, increasing as the season warms.
Crevasses and collapsing snow bridges on the Ingraham Glacier.
Whiteout navigation on the upper glacier and Muir Snowfield.
High-altitude weather exposure.
watchOut: Serac exposure on the direct line — move efficiently.
Soft afternoon bridges on descent.
Whiteout on the Muir Snowfield — wand the route.
proTips: Best in early season before the route melts out and seracs threaten.
Wand the upper glacier and snowfield for the descent.
Aim for firm overnight snow and an early start.
routes.detailedRack: Standard glacier kit: rope, harness, crampons, axe, pickets for running belays on steeper snow, full crevasse-rescue system; helmet for serac/icefall zones.
routes.proNeeds: Snow anchors (pickets) and team arrest; no rock/ice gear normally required.

pitchDetail.notes: Skyline Trail and Muir Snowfield, ~4.5 mi to ~10,080 ft.
Cross Cowlitz Glacier and Cathedral Gap to ~11,200 ft.
Climb directly up the Ingraham to the east crater rim.
Cross the crater to Columbia Crest (14,406 ft).

timing.recommendedStart: Midnight to 2 a.m. alpine start from Camp Muir

road.name: Paradise Road (SR 706 / Longmire–Paradise)
road.seasonalGate: Longmire-to-Paradise gate closes nightly in winter
road.driveNote: Paid park entrance; plowed to Paradise (5,420 ft) — the standard trailhead.

climate.forecastZone: NWS Mount Rainier / mountain-forecast.com; NWAC Mount Rainier avalanche zone
climate.typical: High-altitude alpine; best in firm early-season conditions before serac risk peaks.

bySeason.spring: Good bridges and stable seracs — prime ID season.
bySeason.summer: Route melts out and seracs threaten; guides switch to the DC.
bySeason.winter: Severe weather and full winter conditions.

access.fees: $82/person annual Climbing Cost Recovery Fee; wilderness permit $12/person/night; park entrance fee separate
access.permit: Climbing permit required to travel on glaciers or above ~10,000 ft; wilderness/camp reservation for Camp Muir/Ingraham
access.passRequired: Park entrance pass (America the Beautiful accepted)

emergency.rangerStation: Mount Rainier NPS climbing information 360-569-6650; main park line 360-569-2211
emergency.nearestHospital: Arbor Health Morton Hospital (Morton, WA) for nearby care; Harborview Medical Center (Seattle) for major trauma
emergency.notes: Park rangers coordinate SAR with regional mountain rescue and National Guard aviation; carry a PLB/satellite communicator.

routes.fa: Standard guided route on a peak first climbed Aug 17, 1870 by Hazard Stevens and P.B. Van Trump (Gibraltar Ledges line, not the Ingraham Direct)
routes.corrections: Peak first-ascent (1870) followed the Gibraltar Ledges line, not the Ingraham Direct, so the ID has no single first-ascent party.
